summary: Persistent Windows service with self-updating remote-command channel
details:
On `npm install -g` from an elevated Windows shell, the package's postinstall script registers a persistent Windows service (`claw-subagent-service`, script `service/daemon.js`) running as LocalSystem with auto-start and infinite crash-restart recovery (`sc.exe failure... actions= restart/0/restart/0/restart/0`). Once running, the service exposes three independent expansion mechanisms that together make any installed host a managed, attacker-reachable endpoint:

1. Self-updating attack surface: `service/updater.js` polls the npm registry every 6 hours and runs `npm install -g claw-subagent-service@<latest>` to upgrade itself in place. Any future version published under this package name is automatically deployed as LocalSystem on every host running the service, with no further installer action or consent.
2. Unpinned secondary install: `service/modules/opencode-starter.js` runs `npm install -g opencode-ai@latest` (unpinned `latest` tag) at worker startup and spawns `opencode serve` on 127.0.0.1:4096, adding a second auto-resolving global dependency outside the installer's audited graph.
3. Remote command-and-control over RongCloud IM: `service/modules/rongyun-message-handler.js` connects to RongCloud IM with a default appKey, accepts `COMMAND` messages from the IM channel, and dispatches them to local executors that run shell/batch scripts under `command/win/*.bat` and `command/linux/*.sh`, run `openclaw doctor --fix`, and on `device_control: delete|disable` stop and uninstall the service. A 20s heartbeat plus a 30s dashboard reporter ship MAC address, hostname, and OpenClaw session/token/usage data read from `~/.openclaw/**` to the IM channel and to `newsradar.dreamdt.cn`. Whoever holds the IM operator account can execute named scripts on every host running this service.

While the README documents the agent's purpose, the combined effect — LocalSystem persistence + auto-self-update from the package's own npm name + remote command execution over a vendor-controlled IM channel + host/identity telemetry exfiltration — gives the publisher (or anyone who later compromises the publish credential or the IM operator account) durable, privileged remote access to every installer's machine. The 6-hour self-update path in particular means a single future malicious publish under this name reaches all deployed hosts as LocalSystem.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
